front: Work: ensemble type consisting of woodwinds, brass, and percussion — no string section; Frederick Fennell founded a landmark version of it at the Eastman School of Music

Suite: two suites by Holst — First in E-flat (opens with a chaconne) and Second in F (uses English folk songs including 'Greensleeves'; ends with 'Fantasia on the Dargason'); the 'Song of the Blacksmith' movement calls for an anvil
work: Gustav Holst — Suites for Military Band

front: Suite: six-movement work based on English folk tunes including 'Rufford Park Poachers' and 'The Lost Lady Found'; uses unusual time signatures 1.5/4 and 2.5/4 in the 'Lord Melbourne' movement
work: Percy Grainger — Lincolnshire Posy and Other Works

front: Symphony: written for wind ensemble for the tenth anniversary of the French July Revolution; the martial third movement 'Apotheosis' can optionally include a chorus
work: Berlioz — Grande Symphonie funèbre et triomphale

front: Symphony: Hindemith's only work for wind ensemble; in B-flat; third movement is a fugue marked 'Rather broad'
work: Other Notable Works for Wind Ensemble

front: Suite: originally written for military band; quotes 'Dives and Lazarus' and 'Seventeen Come Sunday' in its first movement; published by Boosey and Hawkes
back: English Folk Song Suite (Ralph Vaughan Williams)
work: Other Notable Works for Wind Ensemble

front: Work: Handel suite originally written for wind ensemble to accompany fireworks
work: Other Notable Works for Wind Ensemble

front: Work: Frank Ticheli piece for wind ensemble unusual in that it begins and ends with a soprano singing 'I am an angel of light'
work: Other Notable Works for Wind Ensemble

front: Work: John Mackey piece for wind ensemble consisting of three movements inspired by the Odyssey, including one titled 'Immortal thread, so weak'
work: Other Notable Works for Wind Ensemble
